article_title: Microsoft Defender 0-Day Vulnerability “RedSun” Enables Full SYSTEM Access
core_thesis: A newly disclosed, unpatched zero-day vulnerability in Microsoft Defender, dubbed RedSun, allows an unprivileged attacker to escalate privileges to full SYSTEM-level access on fully patched Windows systems by exploiting a logic flaw in Defender's cloud file handling mechanism. The attack uses NTFS directory junctions and opportunistic locks to redirect Defender's file restoration process, overwriting a critical system binary with SYSTEM privileges.
detailed_summary: The article reports on RedSun, a zero-day local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability in Microsoft Defender discovered by researcher Chaotic Eclipse. It is the second such exploit published in April 2026, following BlueHammer (CVE-2026-33825) which Microsoft patched. RedSun exploits a logic flaw: when Defender detects a malicious file with a cloud tag, it rewrites the file to its original location instead of quarantining it. The attack chain involves writing an EICAR test file via the Cloud Files API (cldapi.dll), using an opportunistic lock (oplock) to pause Defender's restoration, creating NTFS directory junctions to redirect the write target to C:\Windows\System32, and overwriting a system binary (e.g., TieringEngineService.exe) with SYSTEM-level privileges. The exploit works reliably on fully patched Windows 10, 11, and Server 2019/2022/2025. The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-33825 (CVSS 7.8, High) and is currently unpatched. No public PoC code has been released, but the methodology is documented on GitHub. Security teams are advised to monitor for anomalous Defender file write activity involving cldapi.dll targeting System32 and to implement detection rules for oplock-assisted file redirection.

entities.name: Chaotic Eclipse
entities.role: Security researcher who discovered and published the RedSun exploit

entities.name: Will Dormann
entities.role: Principal vulnerability analyst at Tharros who confirmed the exploit's reliability

entities.name: Microsoft Defender
entities.role: Antivirus product containing the RedSun vulnerability

entities.name: CVE-2026-33825
entities.role: CVE identifier assigned to the RedSun vulnerability

entities.name: TieringEngineService.exe
entities.role: Critical system binary overwritten in the exploit to gain SYSTEM privileges

key_data_points: RedSun is an unpatched zero-day LPE vulnerability in Microsoft Defender that grants full SYSTEM access on fully patched Windows 10, 11, and Server 2019+ systems (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: provides a reliable privilege escalation vector for gaining highest-level access on target systems).

The exploit leverages a logic flaw where Defender rewrites a cloud-tagged malicious file to its original location instead of quarantining it, allowing redirection via NTFS junctions (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates how to abuse trusted system components for privilege escalation).

The attack chain uses the Cloud Files API (cldapi.dll), opportunistic locks, and NTFS directory junctions to overwrite TieringEngineService.exe with SYSTEM privileges (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: provides a step-by-step methodology that can be adapted for custom exploits).

The vulnerability carries a CVSS score of 7.8 (High) and is classified under CWE: Insufficient Granularity of Access Control, with MITRE ATT&CK mapping to Privilege Escalation (TA0004) (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: helps in understanding the vulnerability classification for reporting and detection evasion).

No public PoC code has been released, but the exploit methodology is documented on GitHub, allowing security researchers to develop detection rules (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: enables creation of custom detection bypasses or weaponization once PoC is available).
methodology_steps: Write an EICAR test file to the filesystem using the Windows Cloud Files API (cldapi.dll) to trigger Defender's cloud file handling.
Set an opportunistic lock (oplock) on the file to pause Defender's restoration process mid-operation.
Create NTFS directory junctions and reparse points to redirect the write target path to C:\Windows\System32.
Allow Defender to resume; it follows the redirected path and overwrites a critical system binary (e.g., TieringEngineService.exe) with SYSTEM-level privileges.
Execute the overwritten binary to obtain full SYSTEM-level code execution.
conclusions: RedSun is a critical unpatched vulnerability that reliably escalates privileges to SYSTEM on fully patched Windows systems, making it a high-value target for red team operations and a significant risk for enterprise environments.

The exploit's reliance on Defender's cloud file handling and NTFS features highlights architectural weaknesses that may be exploited in future attacks, emphasizing the need for defense-in-depth beyond patching.
Until Microsoft releases a patch, security teams must rely on monitoring and detection rules for anomalous Defender file write activity and oplock-assisted redirection to mitigate the risk.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming interests as it details a reliable, unpatched privilege escalation exploit (RedSun) that grants SYSTEM access on fully patched Windows systems. The technical breakdown of the attack chain—using Cloud Files API, opportunistic locks, and NTFS junctions—provides actionable techniques you can incorporate into your offensive security toolkit. As a programmer, understanding the logic flaw and the exploitation mechanics can help you develop custom exploits or detection bypasses. The article also offers guidance on monitoring and detection, which is useful for both offensive and defensive perspectives. Given your ambition, you could experiment with the documented methodology in a lab environment to create a working PoC or integrate it into your red teaming arsenal.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a lab with Windows 11 fully patched to April 2026 and attempt to reproduce the RedSun exploit using the documented methodology. This will give you hands-on experience with NTFS junction abuse and oplock techniques, which are valuable for both privilege escalation and evasion. Additionally, develop custom detection rules (e.g., Sigma or YARA) for the specific indicators (cldapi.dll writes to System32, oplock events) to improve your defensive skills and understand how to bypass such detections.
